refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the acceptance criterion (≥0.999 availability), specifies the measurement scope (bidirectional link), and defines the evaluation period. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to equipment immobilization or unsafe states.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during the evaluation period; calculate availability as (total_time_link_up) / (total_evaluation_time). Log link state transitions with timestamps. Compare measured availability against the 0.999 threshold at evaluation conclusion.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall implement redundant communication paths to minimize single-point-of-failure impact on link availability.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall recover from transient link interruptions and re-establish bidirectional command-telemetry flow.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link availability metrics for post-evaluation analysis and reporting.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement dual independent communication channels (primary and backup) with automatic failover logic; monitor link health continuously and switch to backup upon primary failure detection.
DP-B: Deploy a single high-reliability communication link with built-in error correction and retransmission; rely on carrier-grade network infrastructure (e.g., dedicated LTE or 5G slice).
DP-C: Implement a mesh network topology with multiple relay nodes; route teleoperation traffic dynamically to avoid congestion and single points of failure.
DP-D: Combine dual channels (DP-A) with mesh-capable relay infrastructure (DP-C) and carrier-grade link quality monitoring; prioritize teleoperation traffic over non-critical data.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a hybrid architecture combining dual independent communication channels with automatic failover, mesh-capable relay infrastructure for path diversity, and carrier-grade link quality monitoring with traffic prioritization for teleoperation commands and telemetry.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation link availability to network infrastructure design (carrier selection, relay placement), which is a design domain concern but not a functional coupling—the FR is satisfied regardless of which carrier or relay topology is chosen, as long as the 0.999 availability target is met.

DP-D couples to safety services (SafetyAlert interface) for link-loss notification, but this is a logical dependency, not a functional coupling—safety services can be designed independently to consume the link-state signal.
DP-D couples to vehicle control (Tasking interface) for command injection, but this is a standard producer-consumer relationship, not a functional coupling.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is decoupled from the specific choice of carrier, relay hardware, or failover algorithm. The FR is satisfied by any realization that achieves 0.999 availability. No information flow reversal or circular dependency detected.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Dual communication channels can be provisioned independently (e.g., LTE + 5G, or LTE + satellite) without excessive cost or latency penalty.
Assumption: Mesh relay infrastructure is available or can be deployed at the worksite.

Risk: If both channels fail simultaneously (e.g., due to common-mode failure such as power loss at the base station), availability target cannot be met. Mitigation: Ensure channels use geographically diverse infrastructure and independent power supplies.
Risk: Failover latency may exceed safety-critical timing requirements. Mitigation: Specify maximum failover time and validate against vehicle control response requirements.
Risk: Mesh relay nodes introduce additional points of failure. Mitigation: Design relay network with sufficient redundancy and monitor relay health.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) maximum acceptable failover latency (e.g., <100 ms), (2) minimum number of independent channels required (e.g., 2 primary + 1 backup), (3) relay node redundancy strategy (e.g., N+1 or N+2), and (4) link quality thresholds for triggering failover (e.g., packet loss >5% or latency >500 ms).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the design, implementation, and operation of dual communication channels, failover logic, mesh relay coordination, link quality monitoring, and telemetry/tasking flow control. It interfaces with Vehicle (for Tasking injection and Telemetry consumption), Safety Services (for SafetyAlert notification on link loss), and external Network Link infrastructure.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module sends operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) to Vehicle module via Tasking interface. Failover logic ensures Tasking continuity during channel switching.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives vehicle state, sensor data, and operational metrics from Vehicle module via Telemetry interface. Link availability is measured by monitoring Telemetry flow continuity.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module notifies Safety Services of link loss events, link recovery, and link quality degradation via SafetyAlert interface. Safety Services uses this to trigger safe-state procedures (e.g., equipment immobilization).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module manages dual independent Network Link connections (primary and backup), monitors link health, and orchestrates failover. Network Link is external infrastructure (carrier network, relay mesh) not owned by Teleoperation but managed via this interface.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic, failover orchestration, and channel management. All core DP-D functions (dual channels, failover, mesh coordination, link monitoring) are contained within this module. Minor diffusion: link quality thresholds may be tuned by Safety Services or Vehicle based on operational feedback.

Safety Services is responsible for safe-state procedures triggered by link loss, not for achieving link availability itself. Assigning DP-D to Safety Services would scatter communication logic across modules and violate separation of concerns. Link availability is a communication concern, not a safety concern (though safety depends on it).

Vehicle module owns vehicle control, propulsion, and tool actuation. Assigning link availability to Vehicle would couple communication infrastructure to vehicle mechanics, violating modularity. Vehicle should be agnostic to how commands arrive (single link, dual link, mesh, etc.).

NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. While it could theoretically own link availability, doing so would bypass the Teleoperation module and create diffusion of communication logic across the system. Teleoperation is the appropriate sub-module for this concern.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-D (dual channels, failover, mesh coordination, link monitoring) because: (1) it is the logical home for communication infrastructure and link management, (2) it has clear interfaces to Vehicle (Tasking/Telemetry) and Safety Services (SafetyAlert), (3) it minimizes diffusion of link availability logic across the system, and (4) it enables independent testing and evolution of communication strategies without affecting vehicle or safety logic.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become a bottleneck if it must coordinate with multiple external systems (Vehicle, Safety Services, Network Link) for every link state change. Mitigation: Design asynchronous event-driven interfaces (e.g., SafetyAlert published on link loss, not polled).

Risk: Failover latency may be unacceptable if Teleoperation module is deployed on a distant server. Mitigation: Ensure Teleoperation logic runs on a low-latency platform (e.g., edge compute near the vehicle or base station).

Risk: Mesh relay coordination may require real-time network topology updates, which could exceed Teleoperation module's processing capacity. Mitigation: Delegate relay topology management to a dedicated network orchestration service and expose it via Network Link interface.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the deployment topology: (1) Is Teleoperation logic co-located with the vehicle (on-board), at the base station (remote), or split across both? (2) What is the maximum acceptable latency for failover detection and execution? (3) Who owns the mesh relay infrastructure—Teleoperation, a separate Network Operations module, or external infrastructure? Refine the Network Link interface to specify link health metrics (latency, packet loss, signal strength) and failover triggers.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (dual channels, failover, mesh coordination, link monitoring) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve ≥0.999 link availability). The DP is sufficient to meet the FR: redundancy reduces single-point-of-failure risk, failover ensures continuity, mesh provides path diversity, and monitoring enables measurement. No information flow reversal or circular dependency. DP-D is implementable and testable.

diffusion_risks: Low risk: Teleoperation module is the natural owner of DP-D. Containment score 0.95 indicates minimal diffusion. No other module is a better candidate.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module cleanly encapsulates dual channel management, failover logic, and link monitoring. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and do not leak implementation details. Ownership is clear: Teleoperation owns the DP, Vehicle and Safety Services consume the results via interfaces.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Link availability can be measured directly by monitoring link state transitions and calculating uptime ratio. Teleoperation module can log link events and expose availability metrics via Telemetry interface. Verification is objective and quantifiable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: High. FR-NGHE-010 is allocated to Teleoperation module. Sub-FRs (link loss detection, redundancy, recovery, logging) are allocated to specific Teleoperation sub-components (failover logic, mesh coordinator, link monitor, metrics logger). Allocation is traceable and testable.

recommended_revisions: Refine sub-FR-NGHE-010.1 (link loss detection latency) to specify a maximum acceptable value (e.g., <100 ms) and validate against vehicle control response requirements.
Refine sub-FR-NGHE-010.2 (redundant paths) to specify the minimum number of independent channels and the diversity requirement (e.g., geographic, carrier, technology).
Refine sub-FR-NGHE-010.3 (recovery) to specify maximum recovery time and acceptable data loss during failover.
Refine sub-FR-NGHE-010.4 (logging) to specify log retention, format, and accessibility for post-evaluation analysis.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (link loss detection, redundancy, recovery, logging). DP-D is sound and well-allocated to Teleoperation module. The FR-DP-IM chain passes all gates. However, each sub-FR requires further decomposition to specify design parameters (e.g., failover algorithm, relay topology, link quality thresholds) and implementation modules (e.g., failover controller, mesh coordinator, link monitor). Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to achieve sufficient design detail for implementation.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds of link failure.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall maintain at least [N] independent communication paths (primary, backup, mesh) to minimize single-point-of-failure impact.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall recover from transient link interruptions and re-establish bidirectional command-telemetry flow within [Y] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link availability metrics (uptime, downtime, failover events, link quality) for post-evaluation analysis and reporting.

assumptions: Dual communication channels can be provisioned independently without excessive cost or latency penalty.
Mesh relay infrastructure is available or can be deployed at the worksite.
Failover latency <100 ms is acceptable for vehicle control safety.
Link quality thresholds (e.g., packet loss >5%, latency >500 ms) are sufficient to trigger failover before link loss is perceived by the operator.
Teleoperation module is deployed on a low-latency platform (edge compute or on-board).
Safety Services can respond to SafetyAlert notifications within acceptable time bounds to execute safe-state procedures.
open_risks: Common-mode failure risk: If both primary and backup channels fail simultaneously (e.g., power loss at base station), availability target cannot be met. Mitigation strategy needed.
Failover latency risk: If failover takes >100 ms, vehicle control may be disrupted. Validation against vehicle control response requirements needed.
Mesh relay reliability risk: Relay nodes introduce additional points of failure. Relay network redundancy strategy needed.
Link quality threshold tuning risk: Thresholds may be too aggressive (false failovers) or too lenient (delayed failover). Tuning and validation needed.
Deployment topology risk: Unclear whether Teleoperation logic is on-board, remote, or split. This affects latency and reliability. Clarification needed.
evidence_needed: Carrier network availability data for primary and backup channels (e.g., LTE and 5G uptime statistics).
Mesh relay topology design and redundancy analysis (e.g., N+1 or N+2 relay coverage).
Failover algorithm specification and latency measurements (e.g., detection time + switching time).
Link quality threshold justification (e.g., packet loss and latency thresholds that correlate with operator-perceived link loss).
Vehicle control response time requirements (e.g., maximum acceptable command latency and telemetry update rate).
Safety Services response time requirements (e.g., maximum acceptable time to execute safe-state procedures after SafetyAlert).
Deployment topology decision (on-board vs. remote vs. split) and latency budget allocation.
Post-evaluation availability measurement methodology and data collection plan.
